blocks.content: He'd almost said it. The name. He'd stopped himself at the last second — not because he didn't know, but because saying it out loud to Davenport made it real in a way that couldn't be undone. And real had consequences he wasn't ready to trigger yet.
He stood at the window. The house next door. Kitchen light on. A figure moving — making tea or coffee, the quiet efficiency of someone going through a morning routine.
He watched. That was what he did. He watched and he read and he waited for the thing that would turn a direction into a certainty.
The IP address is one data point. Behavioral analysis is not evidence. Wren's uncertain memory is not evidence. I need the tox. I need the wire. I need something that a courtroom can hold.
Until then — a direction. That's all I call it. A direction.
He turned back to the legal pad.
Page twenty-three. The page he'd turned over last night with the circle and the name inside it.
